div.head: CO–37. Memorandum from the Office in Charge of Colombian Affairs (Gerberich) to the Deputy Assistant Secretary of State for Inter-American Affairs (Barall)Source: Department of State, ARA/WST Files, Lot 64 D 1, “Colombia 1960.” Official Use Only. This memorandum was sent through Samuel O. Lane, Deputy Director of the Office of West Coast Affairs.

list.head: SUBJECT
list.item:
Colombian Excess Sugar.In a letter to Gerberich, October 28, 1960, Milton K. Wells, Counselor of Embassy in Colombia, stated in part the following:
“Over the last week we have received two or three Memoranda of Conversation in regard to Ambassador Sanz de Santamaria’s efforts to seal the Department on the idea that unless we buy Colombian surplus sugar social chaos will result. This is an extremely exaggerated idea. In any case, I am sure the matter is being given deliberate, rather than hasty, consideration in the Department.” (ARA/WST) Files, Lot 64 D 1, “Colombia 1960.”

div.p:
Mr. Rourk, Mrs. Mulliken and I have about exhausted all possibilities for helping the Colombians to dispose of the 40,000 tons of excess sugar in the United States. We have investigated the following: 1.
Purchase under the Sugar Act.Reference is to the Sugar Act of 1948, as amended and extended, particularly the amendments of July 6, 1960 (Public Law 86–592); for text, see 74 State 330. (Colombia has never joined the International Sugar Agreement.)For text of the International Sugar Agreement, opened for signature at London, December 1, 1958, and entered into force for the United States, October 9, 1959, see 10 UST (pt. 3) 2189. 2.
DLF financing. 3.
ICA purchase. 4.
Purchase for stockpile. 5.
Purchase under Defense Production Act.For text of the Defense Production Act of 1950 (Public Law 774), approved September 8, 1950, see 64 State 798.
The results of these investigations have resulted in the conclusion that there is no legal way in which we can buy this sugar in 1960.

There remain, apparently, only two alternatives: 1.
Colombia to finance its excess stocks until next spring, meanwhile taking steps to join the ISA. Although there is a strong possibility that we can buy Colombian sugar under the Sugar Act, when it is renewed on March 31, it is impossible to guarantee this. 2.
Colombia to sell the sugar at world market prices.

p.hi: It is recommended that Mr. Mann tell this to Ambassador Santamaria, if he has not already done so.
p.note: The following notation by Deputy Assistant Secretary Barall appears on the source text: “Mr. Mann agreed to call Amb. S. He will say no on above but indicate Col. may be able to sell sugar to U.S. next year if ISA members (quota countries) unable meet needs of U.S.”
